## 🔍 `rehype-sanitize` — Overview & hast Compatibility

### 📦 What is `rehype-sanitize`?

`rehype-sanitize` is a unified (rehype) plugin to make sure HTML is safe. It drops anything that isn't explicitly allowed by a schema, defaulting to how GitHub.com works.

It is part of the **unified** ecosystem, which transforms content with abstract syntax trees (ASTs). **rehype** adds support for HTML to unified, and **hast** is the HTML AST that rehype uses — making `rehype-sanitize` a plugin that directly transforms the hast tree.

---

### 🧱 hast Compatibility

`rehype-sanitize` is built on **`hast-util-sanitize`**, which cleans hast syntax trees.

The package declares a peer/direct dependency of **`"hast-util-sanitize": "^5.0.0"`**, and is developed alongside **`rehype@^13.0.0`** and **`typescript@^5.0.0`**.

The plugin works with **rehype-parse version 3+**, **rehype-stringify version 3+**, **rehype version 5+**, and **unified version 6+**.

Types for hast are available in `@types/hast`. Projects maintained by the unified collective are compatible with maintained versions of Node.js, and when a new major release is cut, support for unmaintained Node.js versions is dropped.

---

### ⚙️ Installation & ESM

This package is **ESM only**. In Node.js (version 16+), install it with npm. It can also be used in browsers via `https://esm.sh/rehype-sanitize@6?bundle`.

---

### 🛡️ Security

The defaults are safe, but improper use of `rehype-sanitize` can open you up to a **cross-site scripting (XSS) attack**. You should use `rehype-sanitize` **after** the last unsafe thing in your pipeline.

**DOM clobbering** is another attack vector where malicious HTML confuses an application by naming elements via `id` or `name` attributes so they shadow properties in the global `window` scope. This commonly occurs when user content is used to generate heading IDs.

To address this, `rehype-sanitize` solves the clobbering problem by **prefixing every `id` and `name` attribute** with `'user-content-'`.

---

### 🗂️ Schema & API

The package exports the identifier **`defaultSchema`**, and the default export is **`rehypeSanitize`**. The default schema follows **GitHub-style sanitation**.

The schema option is advanced as it requires knowledge of syntax trees — see the docs for `Schema` in `hast-util-sanitize` for full details.

---

### 📊 Project Health (as of 2026)

According to Socket.dev, `rehype-sanitize` demonstrated a **not healthy version release cadence** because the last version was released over a year ago. It has **2 open source maintainers** collaborating on the project.

**F1 -- CVE transitive dependency pinning (commit a0516c3):**
- Added `overrides` to `package.json` pinning `picomatch@^4.0.3`, `uuid@^11`, `ajv@^8.17.1`.

**F3 -- Compodoc output redirection (commit a5ea722):**

- `documentation.json` was not in the git index so `git rm --cached` was not needed.

**F4 -- Stored-XSS defense (commit 6f4bcb9):**
- `CONTRIBUTING.md`: created with JSDoc policy section forbidding HTML tags and `javascript:` hrefs, explaining the XSS risk, directing to plain text/Markdown.

content.thinking:
The advisor flagged several issues:

1. **ESLint selector fix**: The trailing `\*$` in the Block selector is wrong -- it would reject valid JSDoc blocks ending in non-asterisk. I need to drop the trailing anchor. Also point 1 says "Current selector matches `/* <foo> */` non-JSDoc blocks too" -- but wait, the selector I used was `Block[value=/^\*[\s\S]*<[a-zA-Z][\s\S]*\*$/]`. 

The `value` of a Block AST node is the content BETWEEN `/*` and `*/`. 

So for `/** text */`, the value would be `* text ` (starts with `*`), and for `/* text */`, the value would be ` text ` (starts with space). The `^\*` pattern should correctly distinguish JSDoc blocks from regular comments. But the trailing `\*$` is the real problem -- it requires the value to end with `*`, which JSDoc blocks don't do since the value stops before the closing `*/`. I need to reconsider what the actual structure looks like here.

Looking at how the AST represents these comments, a JSDoc block like `/** description */` has a value of `* description ` (with a trailing space), and multi-line JSDoc blocks have values like `*\n * description\n ` -- they start with `*` but never end with one. So the regex pattern with the trailing `\*$` anchor won't match these cases. The advisor is right that I should drop the trailing anchor and use something like `Block[value=/^\*[\s\S]*<[a-zA-Z]/]` instead to catch JSDoc blocks containing HTML tags. The trailing `\*$` anchor is the real problem—for JSDoc blocks ending with a space, it won't match at all, so the rule fails to catch HTML tags in those cases. I need to remove that anchor and use `Block[value=/^\*[\s\S]*<[a-zA-Z]/]` instead, which will match any block comment starting with `*` and containing an HTML tag opening.

On the sanitization front, the advisor's concern about Compodoc is valid—`rehypeSanitize` only protects the MDX rendering path, but Compodoc extracts JSDoc descriptions into `documentation.json`, and Storybook renders those descriptions through its own component outside the MDX pipeline, so that sanitization doesn't apply there. I need to research how Storybook's Angular integration actually renders those Compodoc descriptions to determine if there's a real XSS vulnerability there, then tackle both the ESLint selector fix and this potential sanitization gap.

Here is a comprehensive summary of the search results related to **Storybook 10, Angular, Compodoc, JSDoc, HTML rendering in the Docs tab, and XSS/`innerHTML` sanitization**:

---

## 🔍 Storybook + Angular + Compodoc: JSDoc, HTML Rendering & XSS in the Docs Tab

---

### 📖 Compodoc & JSDoc Integration

To make the most out of Storybook in your Angular project, you can set up Compodoc integration. You can include JSDoc comments above components, directives, and other parts of your Angular code to include documentation for those elements, and Compodoc uses these comments to generate documentation for your application.

In Storybook, it is useful to add explanatory comments above `@Inputs` and `@Outputs`, since these are the main elements that Storybook displays in its user interface — the elements you can interact with, such as controls.

When used with Storybook, Compodoc can automatically generate documentation for your Angular components by analyzing their TypeScript code, decorators, and JSDoc comments.

---

### ⚙️ Compodoc Setup (Built-in in `@storybook/angular`)

When installing Storybook via `npx storybook@latest init`, you can set up Compodoc automatically. If you have already installed Storybook, you can set up Compodoc manually.

Note that Compodoc is now built into `@storybook/angular`; you don't have to call it explicitly.

---

### 📝 Descriptions in the Docs Tab

The Description block displays the description for a component, story, or meta, obtained from their respective JSDoc comments.

Descriptions are pulled from the JSDoc comments or parameters, and they are rendered as Markdown. See the "Writing descriptions" section for more details.

To describe a story via parameters instead of comments, add it to `parameters.docs.description.story`; to describe meta/component, add it to `parameters.docs.description.component`. JSDoc comments are recommended, and the `parameters.docs.description.X` properties should only be used in situations where comments are not possible, or where you want the description in Storybook to differ from the comments.

---

### ⚠️ XSS / HTML Rendering Issue with JSDoc & `innerHTML`

A known bug exists where Storybook Docs renders the actual markup inside JSDoc comments when it shouldn't. For example, SB Docs' PropTable renders the real markup for `<a>` and `<button>` instead of the string. The expected behavior is that when you comment `<button>` in JSDoc, it should render as `<button>` in Storybook Docs, **not** as a real DOM element.

This is a direct XSS/`innerHTML` concern: Storybook was injecting unsanitized HTML from JSDoc descriptions directly into the DOM. This can be a security risk and also causes unintended rendering.

One of the first things found when writing JSDoc for Storybook is that either Compodoc or Storybook does not escape quotation marks properly for displaying in web browsers. A workaround is to use the HTML escape for apostrophes and quotes — `'` and `"` — inside JSDoc documentation.

---

### 🛡️ JSDoc Tag Support in Compodoc

Compodoc only supports a small number of JSDoc tags: `@returns`, `@ignore`, `@param`, `@link`, and `@example`. The most useful of these in Storybook is `@ignore`, which removes the property from the Storybook Controls panel — helpful for hiding properties and functions that external components should not use.

---

### 📄 Compodoc Output (`documentation.json`)

When Compodoc builds, it creates a large file in the root directory of the application called `documentation.json`. This file is created when Compodoc is run, does not need to be committed to git, and can safely be included in a `.gitignore` file.

---

### 🔑 Key Takeaways

| Topic | Detail |
|---|---|
| **JSDoc → Docs Tab** | JSDoc comments on `@Input`/`@Output` flow through Compodoc → `documentation.json` → Storybook Docs tab |
| **HTML in JSDoc** | Storybook may render raw HTML from JSDoc via `innerHTML`, causing XSS-like rendering bugs |
| **Workaround** | Escape HTML entities (`<`, `>`, `"`) in JSDoc comments |
| **Descriptions** | Use `parameters.docs.description.component` or JSDoc block comments |
| **Compodoc built-in** | As of recent Storybook for Angular versions, Compodoc is built into `@storybook/angular` |

If you need to dig deeper into a specific aspect (e.g., a sanitization fix or a Storybook 10-specific changelog item), feel free to ask!
